title: Quiz: Classical Fisher Information
description: Test your understanding of Fisher information and its role in statistical estimation and natural gradients

questions.question: What does Fisher information measure?

options.text: The total amount of data available
How sensitive a probability distribution is to parameter changes
The number of parameters in a model

questions.explanation: Fisher information I(θ) = E[(∂ log p / ∂θ)²] measures how much the log-likelihood changes with the parameter, quantifying the distribution's sensitivity to parameter perturbations.

questions.question: What does the Cramér-Rao bound state?

options.text: The variance of any estimator equals the Fisher information
The variance of any unbiased estimator is at least 1/I(θ)

questions.explanation: The Cramér-Rao bound states that Var(θ̂) ≥ 1/I(θ) for any unbiased estimator. This sets a fundamental limit on estimation precision.

questions.question: How is the natural gradient related to the standard gradient?
questions.explanation: The natural gradient is computed as ∇̃L = I(θ)⁻¹ ∇L, where I(θ) is the Fisher information matrix. This transforms the gradient to account for parameter space geometry.

questions.question: What is the Fisher information for Bernoulli(p)?
questions.explanation: For Bernoulli distribution, I(p) = 1/(p(1-p)). This is highest near p=0 or p=1, meaning extreme probabilities carry more information.

questions.question: The Fisher information matrix must be positive semi-definite.
questions.explanation: True. Since FIM = E[score × scoreᵀ], it's an outer product expectation and thus always positive semi-definite.

questions.question: What advantage does natural gradient descent have over standard gradient descent?

options.text: It is parameterization-invariant and respects the geometry of the distribution family
It only works for Gaussian distributions

questions.explanation: Natural gradient is invariant to reparameterization and takes the steepest descent direction in the Riemannian manifold defined by the FIM, respecting the natural geometry.

questions.question: What is the main computational challenge with natural gradient in deep learning?

options.text: Computing the gradient of the loss
Computing and inverting the Fisher information matrix

questions.explanation: The FIM is O(d²) where d is the number of parameters. For neural networks with millions of parameters, this is prohibitively expensive, requiring approximations like diagonal or K-FAC.

questions.question: Which statements about Fisher information are true? (Select all that apply)

options.text: It can be computed as the expected Hessian of negative log-likelihood
It defines a Riemannian metric on parameter space
Higher Fisher information means easier parameter estimation
It is always a scalar value

questions.explanation: FIM = E[-∂² log p / ∂θ²] (expected negative Hessian), defines Riemannian geometry, and higher I(θ) gives tighter Cramér-Rao bounds. It's a matrix for multiple parameters.
